briefing.narrative:
The monastery's security monk — a paranoid individual who puts locks on locks — is pacing the server room.

"Someone committed our SECRET to the config file. And then someone ELSE committed a perfectly good readme on top of it. We can't just reset — that would destroy the readme commit. We need to surgically UNDO the bad config change without touching anything else."

He slams his fist on the table. "This is why I wanted to use carrier pigeons instead of git. Pigeons don't leak secrets."

The monastery cat disagrees. It has intercepted many pigeons.
briefing.concept: git revert <commit> creates a NEW commit that undoes the changes from a specific commit. Unlike reset, revert doesn't rewrite history — it adds to it. This makes it safe for shared/published branches where you can't rewrite commits others have already pulled.
objectives: Use 'git log' to find the commit that leaked the secret
Revert the bad config change (the 'Oops' commit) using git revert
The config.txt must be restored (debug: false, no SECRET)
The readme.txt must still exist (don't lose later commits)

hints.text: The security monk checks his locks: 'Use git log --oneline to find the bad commit. It says "Oops: bad config change". Note its hash, or use HEAD~1 since it is the second-to-last commit.'
A passing monk in a trenchcoat whispers: 'git revert HEAD~1 will create a new commit that undoes the changes from that specific commit. The readme stays safe.'
The monastery cat types while looking over its shoulder: git revert HEAD~1 --no-edit
